title: MDPs: Teaching a Decision-Maker to Think in States and Rewards
concept: Markov Decision Processes (states, actions, rewards, policies, value functions)
learningObjectives: Define a problem in terms of states, actions, rewards, and a policy
Explain the Markov property and why it matters for whether a problem can be modeled as an MDP
Distinguish a policy from a value function and explain how they relate
Frame battery scheduling as an MDP: what is the state, what are the actions, what is the reward
whyItMatters: Every model so far in this curriculum predicted a value (regression) or a category (classification) from a single snapshot of features — none of them decided what to do next, or accounted for how today's decision affects tomorrow's options. Battery scheduling is fundamentally a sequential decision problem: charging now affects what's possible later, and reinforcement learning is the framework built specifically for that.
capstoneContribution: The formal problem definition for the Digital Twin's battery scheduler — state, action, and reward defined precisely enough that an RL algorithm can actually be applied to it in the following lessons.

problem.summary: Nothing yet decides what the battery should actually do, and today's choice shapes tomorrow's options.
problem.content: Your Mission 3/4 forecasters can predict tomorrow's demand, but nothing yet decides what the battery should actually do about it — and that decision today changes what's possible tomorrow (a battery discharged now can't discharge again until recharged), which none of your previous models had to reason about at all.

motivation.summary: This is a genuinely different kind of problem: what to do, not what will happen.
motivation.content: This is a genuinely different kind of problem from everything else in the curriculum so far: not 'what will happen' but 'what should I do, given that my choice now shapes my options later.' That's exactly what the MDP framework and reinforcement learning exist to formalize and solve.

theory.summary: States, actions, and rewards define an MDP; policies and value functions relate directly.
theory.content: A Markov Decision Process consists of states (a snapshot of everything relevant right now — battery charge level, current demand, current price), actions (what the agent can choose — charge, discharge, hold), and rewards (a number, computed after each action, reflecting how good that action was — e.g. negative cost, penalty for overload). The Markov property assumes the current state contains everything needed to decide the best next action — the past only matters insofar as it's reflected in the current state, not as separate history. A policy (π) maps states to actions (what to do in each situation); a value function estimates the total future reward achievable from a given state (or state-action pair) under a given policy — the two are deeply related, since a good policy is exactly one that chooses actions leading to high-value states.

visualExplanation.summary: The agent-environment loop: state, action, reward, next state, repeated.
visualExplanation.content: An agent in a battery-charge state takes an action, receives a reward, and transitions to a new state, repeated as a looping cycle — the fundamental agent-environment interaction loop every RL algorithm in this skill folder builds on.

interactiveDemo.summary: Step through a simplified battery MDP choosing actions and observing rewards.
interactiveDemo.content: Click through a simplified battery MDP step by step — choosing charge/discharge/hold at each state, watching the state (charge level) update and a reward number appear, building intuition for the state-action-reward loop before any algorithm is introduced.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: define a simple state, action function, and reward.

independentCoding.summary: Add a safety-reserve penalty to the reward function.
independentCoding.content: Extend the `take_action` function to include a penalty in the reward if the resulting `battery_soc` would go below 0.1 (a safety reserve), and test it against a few different starting states and actions.

debugging.summary: Fix a reward computed from stale, pre-action state.
debugging.content: A reward function computes the reward from the state *before* the action was applied instead of accounting for its actual effect, silently rewarding actions based on stale information. Identify and fix the reward computation order.

reflection.summary: The Markov property holds reasonably well here, but might not always.
reflection.content: Why does the Markov property's assumption — 'the current state has everything needed to decide' — actually hold reasonably well for battery scheduling, but might fail for a problem where recent history matters beyond what's captured in the current snapshot?

teachBack.summary: Explain policy vs. value function using the battery MDP.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone the difference between a policy and a value function using the battery MDP as your concrete example.

miniProject.summary: Define a complete, realistic battery MDP ready for an RL algorithm.
miniProject.content: Define a complete, more realistic battery MDP: a richer state (soc, demand forecast, price, time of day), a documented action space, and a reward function reflecting cost and a safety constraint, ready for an actual RL algorithm to be applied to in the next lesson.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the problem specification every RL algorithm in this skill folder will be trained against.
portfolioEvidence.content: This formal MDP definition is the actual problem specification every RL algorithm in the rest of this skill folder will be trained against.

labs.title: Battery MDP Definition
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise defining a complete battery-scheduling MDP with state, action, and reward function, including a planted stale-state reward bug to fix.

simulations.title: MDP Agent Loop Visualizer
simulations.description: Step-through visualization of the state-action-reward-next-state loop for a simplified battery MDP.

flashcards.front: What does the Markov property assume about a state?
flashcards.back: That the current state contains everything needed to decide the best next action — the past matters only insofar as it's already reflected in the current state, not as separate history to track.

flashcards.front: How does a value function relate to a policy?
flashcards.back: A value function estimates the total future reward achievable from a state (or state-action pair); a good policy is exactly one that consistently chooses actions leading toward high-value states.

rubric: State definition includes everything genuinely needed to decide a good next action, without unnecessary bloat
Action space and reward function are precisely and unambiguously defined, ready for an algorithm to be applied
Correctly identifies and fixes the stale-state reward computation bug

references.title: Sutton & Barto — Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction
